topic.topic: What causes skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?

topic_summary: tree_search_scientist research package for: What causes skill-library bloat in lifelong-learning agents?

primitive_definitions.name: skill
primitive_definitions.definition: A reusable parameterized module or policy specialized for a specific task or subtask, stored in a library for future use.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Skills are the atomic unit of knowledge in lifelong learning; bloat is measured as excessive accumulation of skills.

primitive_definitions.name: experience replay
primitive_definitions.definition: A memory buffer that stores past experiences (state-action-reward-next state tuples) and samples them during training to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: The mechanism under investigation; its design directly influences which old tasks are retained and whether new skills are created.

primitive_definitions.name: catastrophic forgetting
primitive_definitions.definition: The tendency of neural networks to lose performance on previously learned tasks when trained on new tasks, due to parameter interference.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: The problem that experience replay aims to solve; bloat may be an unintended side effect of aggressive retention strategies.

assumptions.assumption: Experience replay that retains all past samples forces the agent to maintain distinct skills for each task variation, preventing skill reuse.
assumptions.rationale: If the agent stores all experiences, it can always rehearse old tasks, so there is no pressure to compress or merge skills; instead, it may create new skills for each subtle change.
assumptions.possible_failure: The agent might still reuse skills if tasks are highly similar, regardless of replay strategy.

assumptions.assumption: Diversity-based selective replay can maintain performance similar to full replay while reducing skill count.
assumptions.rationale: Selecting only novel or diverse samples reduces buffer size while preserving essential information for retention, potentially avoiding unnecessary skill creation.
assumptions.possible_failure: Selective replay might miss critical samples, leading to forgetting or requiring even more skills to compensate.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: Lifelong-learning agents suffer from skill-library bloat because standard experience replay retains all past samples, which implicitly encourages the agent to create separate skills for each minor task variation. By replacing full replay with a diversity-based selective replay mechanism, the agent can maintain performance while reducing skill count, as it no longer over-specializes.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

causal_chain.step: Lifelong-learning agents suffer from skill-library bloat because standard experience replay retains all past samples, which implicitly encourages the agent to create separate skills for each minor task variation. By replacing full replay with a diversity-based selective replay mechanism, the agent can maintain performance while reducing skill count, as it no longer over-specializes.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: Lifelong-learning agents suffer from skill-library bloat because standard experience replay retains all past samples, which implicitly encourages the agent to create separate skills for each minor task variation. By replacing full replay with a diversity-based selective replay mechanism, the agent can maintain performance while reducing skill count, as it no longer over-specializes.

tensions.statement: Balancing retention of past knowledge (avoiding forgetting) against parsimony of the skill library (reducing bloat).
tensions.why_it_matters: While experience replay is known to aid retention, its direct impact on skill library bloat has not been systematically studied. This work provides a causal link between memory management and skill parsimony, offering practical guidance for designing efficient lifelong-learning systems.

candidate_questions.question: Does diversity-based selective replay reduce skill-library bloat without increasing catastrophic forgetting compared to standard experience replay?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: While experience replay is known to aid retention, its direct impact on skill library bloat has not been systematically studied. This work provides a causal link between memory management and skill parsimony, offering practical guidance for designing efficient lifelong-learning systems.

research_question.question: Does diversity-based selective replay reduce skill-library bloat without increasing catastrophic forgetting compared to standard experience replay?
research_question.expected_contribution: While experience replay is known to aid retention, its direct impact on skill library bloat has not been systematically studied. This work provides a causal link between memory management and skill parsimony, offering practical guidance for designing efficient lifelong-learning systems.

hypothesis.statement: Using a selective replay buffer that retains only diverse experiences (e.g., novel with respect to existing skills) will lead to fewer skills and comparable or lower forgetting than using a full replay buffer.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: The selective replay agent will have significantly fewer skills at the end of training while maintaining similar task performance and forgetting levels.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: We will compare two lifelong-learning agents on a sequence of 10 related classification tasks (e.g., variants of CIFAR-10 with different label permutations). Agent A uses standard experience replay (buffer storing all past samples). Agent B uses diversity-based selective replay: after each task, we compute embedding distances for each sample and retain only those that are most dissimilar from already stored skills (e.g., using a threshold on cosine similarity in the feature space). Both agents use the same architecture (e.g., a neural network with a modular skill library). We measure (1) total number of skills created over the sequence, (2) average task accuracy after each task, and (3) forgetting metric (performance drop on previous tasks). We repeat over 5 random seeds.
independent_variables: Replay strategy: full replay vs. diversity-based selective replay
dependent_variables: Average task performance (accuracy) after final task
Forgetting measure (average performance drop on previous tasks)
controls: Same skill creation threshold (if any) except replay strategy
Same number of training epochs per task
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If the selective replay agent does not have significantly fewer skills than the full replay agent, or if it exhibits significantly higher forgetting (e.g., >5% drop), then the hypothesis is falsified.

supports_hypothesis: Selective replay produces fewer skills while maintaining similar task performance and forgetting.
Full replay agent creates many redundant skills for similar tasks.
rejects_hypothesis: Selective replay agent has similar or higher number of skills.
Selective replay agent shows significantly worse forgetting (e.g., >10% performance drop).

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If the hypothesis is falsified, we will investigate the diversity metric. Possibly the embedding space does not capture task distinctions, or the selection threshold is too aggressive. We could test alternative diversity measures (e.g., based on skill gradients) or adaptive thresholds.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Experience replay that retains all past samples forces the agent to maintain distinct skills for each task variation, preventing skill reuse.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Does diversity-based selective replay reduce skill-library bloat without increasing catastrophic forgetting compared to standard experience replay?

branches.research_direction: Investigating redundancy in skill acquisition due to high similarity thresholds for new skill creation.
branches.mechanism_bet: Bloat arises because the agent creates a new skill for every minor task variation, even when existing skills could be reused via adaptation.
branches.minimal_experiment: Compare two lifelong-learning agents on a sequence of related tasks: one that creates skills based on a low similarity threshold vs. one with high threshold. Measure number of skills and performance.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Both agents may perform similarly if tasks are diverse enough, showing bloat is not solely due to threshold but also task homogeneity.

branches.research_direction: Investigating bloat from catastrophic forgetting mitigation strategies that retain old skills.
branches.mechanism_bet: Skill-library bloat is caused by experience replay mechanisms that force the agent to retain all old skills to prevent forgetting, leading to accumulation.
branches.minimal_experiment: Compare two agents: one with experience replay that retains all past samples, and one with selective replay that only retains diverse samples. Measure skill count and forgetting.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Selective replay might still lead to bloat if diversity metric is not well-defined, or might cause forgetting if too aggressive.

branches.research_direction: Investigating bloat due to lack of skill consolidation or abstraction over time.
branches.mechanism_bet: Bloat occurs because the agent never compresses or merges similar skills into higher-level abstractions, leading to many low-level skills.
branches.minimal_experiment: Implement an agent that periodically clusters skills and merges similar ones vs. one that never merges. Compare skill count and performance on held-out tasks.
branches.expected_failure_mode: Merging might degrade performance if clusters are not semantically aligned.

selection.selection_rationale: B002 proposes a novel link between catastrophic forgetting mitigation (experience replay) and skill-library bloat, which is under-explored. The experiment is clear and falsifiable: comparing full vs selective replay. It also has practical implications for designing memory-efficient lifelong-learning systems.
expansion_instructions: Design an experimental setup with a sequence of related tasks (e.g., varying object recognition tasks).

Implement two lifelong-learning agents: one using standard experience replay (retaining all past samples) and one using diversity-based selective replay (e.g., only retaining samples that are novel relative to existing skills).
Define metrics: total number of skills created, average task performance, and degree of catastrophic forgetting (e.g., performance drop on old tasks).
Run experiments over multiple random seeds to ensure statistical significance.
Analyze the trade-off between bloat (number of skills) and forgetting; test if selective replay can reduce bloat without increasing forgetting.
Consider variations of the diversity metric (e.g., similarity threshold in embedding space) and their impact.
